eli3: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) tests whether the means of three or more groups are equal. Instead of doing multiple t-tests (which inflates Type I error), ANOVA performs a single F-test by decomposing total variability into two parts: variance between groups (signal) and variance within groups (noise).

The F-statistic = between-group variance / within-group variance. If all groups have the same mean, both numerator and denominator estimate the same σ², so F ≈ 1. If the groups have different means, the numerator becomes large relative to the denominator, and F is large — evidence against H₀.

A significant ANOVA tells you 'at least one group mean differs' — but not which one. Post-hoc tests (Tukey's HSD, Bonferroni correction) identify which specific pairs differ while controlling the family-wise error rate.

whenWhy.title: Comparing more than two group means simultaneously
whenWhy.body: Conducting k(k−1)/2 pairwise t-tests inflates the Type I error: with 5 groups and α=0.05 per test, the probability of at least one false rejection is 1−(0.95)^10 ≈ 40%. ANOVA controls Type I error at α across all group comparisons by making a single omnibus test.

whenWhy.title: Experimental design: analysing randomised trials
whenWhy.body: ANOVA is the standard analysis for randomised controlled experiments with categorical treatment variables. One-way ANOVA: single factor. Two-way ANOVA: two factors plus their interaction. Repeated-measures ANOVA: same subjects across conditions (equivalent to multivariate t-test). These form the foundation of experimental statistics.

whenWhy.title: Feature importance in regression (Analysis of Variance table)
whenWhy.body: The ANOVA table decomposes SST = SSR + SSE (total = regression + residual sum of squares). The F-test for the overall regression model (H₀: all β = 0) is exactly an ANOVA F-test. R² = SSR/SST is the fraction of variance explained. Every regression summary output includes an ANOVA table.

whenWhy.title: Pre-modelling feature screening
whenWhy.body: One-way ANOVA F-test between a continuous feature and a categorical target is a fast, interpretable feature selection criterion. Scikit-learn's f_classif uses the ANOVA F-test to rank continuous features by their association with a categorical target. High F = feature discriminates between classes.

deepDiveIntro: One-way ANOVA models each observation as a grand mean plus a group-specific offset plus random noise. The null hypothesis is that all group offsets are zero — meaning every group has the same mean. The test partitions the total variation in the data into two parts: how much groups differ from the overall average (between-group) versus how much observations vary within each group (within-group).

The F-statistic is the ratio of between-group variance to within-group variance. Under the null hypothesis both quantities estimate the same underlying variance, so F should be close to 1. When groups genuinely differ, the between-group variance inflates while the within-group variance stays the same, pushing F above 1. The p-value is the probability of seeing an F this large by chance alone.

deepDive.heading: Variance Decomposition
deepDive.body: Total variation in the data is split into two sources: variation between group means (SSB) and variation of individual observations around their own group mean (SSW). Dividing each by its degrees of freedom gives the mean squares MSB and MSW. Under the null hypothesis both estimate the same underlying noise variance. If groups truly differ, MSB becomes inflated because group means are spread further apart than chance would predict. [1]

deepDive.heading: F-Statistic and F-Distribution
deepDive.body: The F-statistic is the ratio of between-group variance to within-group variance. Under the null hypothesis it follows an F-distribution determined by the number of groups and the total sample size. Large F values are unlikely under the null, so a large F gives evidence that at least one group mean differs. For the special case of two groups, F equals the square of the two-sample t-statistic — ANOVA is the direct generalization of the t-test to more than two groups. [1]

deepDive.heading: Assumptions
deepDive.body: ANOVA requires three conditions: observations must be independent within and across groups; values within each group should be approximately normally distributed (though with 30+ samples per group this matters little due to the central limit theorem); and all groups should have similar variance (homoscedasticity). Levene's test checks whether variances are equal. If groups have very different spreads, use Welch's ANOVA, which relaxes the equal-variance requirement. [1]

deepDive.heading: Post-Hoc Tests
deepDive.body: A significant F-test only tells you that at least one group mean differs — it does not say which pair. Post-hoc tests identify which specific pairs are different while controlling the overall false positive rate. Tukey's HSD is the standard choice for all pairwise comparisons. Bonferroni correction is more conservative and simpler. Scheffé allows testing arbitrary contrasts (not just pairs). Dunnett is designed for comparing multiple groups to a single control group. [1][2]

takeaways: F = MSB/MSW; under H₀ ~ F(k−1,n−k); large F → reject equal-means hypothesis. [1]
SST = SSB + SSW: total variance = between-group + within-group. [1]
Assumptions: independence, normality (robust for large n), equal variances (use Welch's ANOVA if violated). [1]
Significant ANOVA → 'at least one mean differs'; post-hoc tests (Tukey's HSD) identify which pairs. [1][2]
For k=2 groups: F = t² — ANOVA is the direct generalisation of the t-test. [1]
ANOVA F-test in regression: H₀: all slope coefficients = 0; R² = SSR/SST = proportion of variance explained. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is Welch's ANOVA and when should you use it?
edgeCases.a: Welch's ANOVA (Welch 1951) relaxes the equal-variance assumption. It uses group-specific variance estimates and computes a modified F-statistic with corrected degrees of freedom (Brown-Forsythe or Welch-Satterthwaite). Use it whenever Levene's test is significant (p < 0.05) or when group sizes are very unequal with different spread. In practice, Welch's ANOVA is a safer default than the classic equal-variance ANOVA. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between one-way and two-way ANOVA?
edgeCases.a: One-way ANOVA: one categorical factor. Two-way ANOVA: two factors, decomposing SST = SS_A + SS_B + SS_AB + SSW where SS_AB is the interaction term. The interaction captures whether the effect of factor A depends on the level of factor B. If the interaction is not significant, you can interpret main effects directly; if significant, main effects must be interpreted conditionally. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 11
Montgomery — Design and Analysis of Experiments (10th ed.), Ch. 3–5
